channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: In Taiwan, ‘Mainland Spouses’ From China Become a Focus of Infiltration Fears
item.description: A Chinese-born woman accused of infiltration has set off a debate over how Taiwan defends its democracy without profiling migrants from China.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley and Amy Chang Chien
item.media:credit: Lam Yik Fei for The New York Times
item.media:description: A village near Taipei. Taiwan officials are confronting what they say is growing Chinese infiltration of the island’s politics, media and internet.

item.title: Where Is Toronto’s Best Party? Try the Basement.
item.description: Millennials who yearn for a bygone era of dancing that feels pure and more organic are bringing back the basement jam, popularized decades ago by Toronto’s Caribbean diaspora.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai and Ian Willms
item.media:description: Unlike most nightclubs, the Caribbean-style basement jams don’t adhere to strict dress-codes, and there are no private booths. The parties hark back to a simpler time.

item.title: Inside the Ebola Epicenter, the Virus Rages With Little to Stop It
item.description: A remote gold mining town is under siege, as medical workers struggle to beat back a surge of deaths and infections.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh and Arlette Bashizi
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times

item.title: Across the Middle East, Muslims Mark Eid Amid War and Crisis
item.description: From Iran to Gaza, Eid al-Adha celebrations were muted as war dragged on and shortages of food and fuel roiled the region.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir and Bilal Shbair
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Palestinians prayed near the ruins of Al-Huda Mosque in Khan Younis, in southern Gaza, on Wednesday, the first day of Eid al-Adha. Israel bombed the mosque early in the war in Gaza.

item.title: A Big Bang, a Fire and Panic as War Enters Romanian Homes
item.description: When a drone crashed into an apartment building in eastern Romania, residents were reminded that the Russia-Ukraine war makes for a dangerous neighbor.
item.dc:creator: Andreea Campeanu and Andrew Higgins
item.media:credit: Andreea Campeanu for The New York Times
item.media:description: A temporary cover for a hole in the roof of a building in Galati, Romania, on Saturday, after a Russian drone slammed into a residential block on Friday.

item.title: Iran’s Team Trains in Limbo for World Cup Overshadowed by War
item.description: The Iranian soccer players, still awaiting visas to the U.S., are practicing in Turkey and making backup plans.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Umit Bektas/Reuters
item.media:description: Members of Iran’s national soccer team practicing in Antalya, Turkey, on Thursday.

item.title: The Russian Drone That Hit Romania Also Hit European Confidence
item.description: The failure to protect NATO territory further increased anxiety about alliance solidarity, Russia’s intentions and Washington’s commitment to collective defense.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Andreea Campeanu for The New York Times
item.media:description: Residents near a building that was struck by Russian drone in Galati, eastern Romania, on Friday.

item.title: Hegseth’s Message to Asian Partners: Do More to Get More
item.description: At a security conference in Singapore, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told Asian military leaders how they could get to “the front of the line” for U.S. assistance.
item.dc:creator: Damien Cave and Chris Buckley
item.media:credit: Jam Sta Rosa/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth at the Shangri-La Dialogue, a security forum in Singapore, on Saturday. Mr. Hegseth described the American approach to security partnerships as “strong, quiet and clear.”

item.title: As Trump Mulls Decision About Iran War Deal, a Restive Middle East Waits to Hear
item.description: The president has wavered on whether to move ahead with an agreement with Iran to end the war. On Friday, he vowed to make a “final determination” soon.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman and Leily Nikounazar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said on Friday that he was meeting with advisers to make a “final determination” about a proposed deal with Iran.

item.title: What to Know About the Ebola Outbreak
item.description: Aid agencies are racing to help underequipped health workers in the Democratic Republic of Congo. More than 245 people are now suspected to have died from the virus.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg, Ephrat Livni and Lynsey Chutel
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Disinfecting a path outside a hospital in Mongbwalu, a mining town at the center of the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

item.title: Ye and Travis Scott Concerts Canceled in Italy Over Security Fears
item.description: The events were canceled “to ensure public order and safety,” the authorities said. Jewish leaders had objected to the concert by Ye, formerly known as Kanye West, who has a history of antisemitic remarks.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg

item.title: Lithuania, Once Occupied by Germany, Is Glad German Troops Are Back
item.description: Berlin has deployed forces to protect the Baltic country amid fears of increased Russian aggression in Europe. This time, the soldiers were welcomed.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:credit: Andrej Vasilenko for The New York Times
item.media:description: German soldiers on their base in Vilnius, Lithuania, in May. The forces deployed in the Baltic country reflect Germany’s increased willingness to act as Europe’s shield amid wavering support from the United States.

item.title: Colombia’s Elections Are a Crucial Test for the Left in Latin America
item.description: Colombia’s leftist candidate for president leads in polls, but his main rival — a far-right outsider who calls himself “The Tiger” — has gained momentum.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Abelardo De La Espriella, a far-right candidate for president of Colombia, closed his campaign in the city of Medellín.

item.title: Who’s the Vegas Retiree With the Big Offer for Greenland?
item.description: Clifford E. Stanley, a retired broker, explains his unusual mission, which caught a lot of people by surprise.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Gettleman and Maya Tekeli
item.media:credit: Oscar Scott Carl for The New York Times
item.media:description: Clifford E. Stanley, a retired mortgage broker, traveled to Nuuk, Greenland, to make residents an offer.

item.title: Edgar Morin, ‘Grandfather’ of French Intellectuals, Dies at 104
item.description: A former member of the Resistance, he went on to a career spanning eras and disciplines. His books and pronouncements carried moral authority.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:credit: Martin Bureau/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Edgar Morin in 2003. “I have a permanent sense of the mystery of all things,” he said. “I still have no idea why I was born, why I exist.”

item.title: What I Saw Inside an Ebola Ward
item.description: Our chief Africa correspondent, Declan Walsh, walked inside an Ebola ward where both adults and children were being treated, and where someone had recently died of the disease amid a spiraling outbreak in the region. This is what he saw.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh, Estelle Caswell, Thomas Vollkommer and Arlette Bashizi

item.title: 5 Are Freed in Laos Cave Rescue After More Than a Week
item.description: Rescuers pumped water from the flooded cave, enabling four miners to crawl out. One used diving gear to escape earlier, and two remain missing.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai
item.media:credit: Association of Volunteers for Lao People, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Miners are tended by rescue worked after being freed on Saturday from a cave in central Laos.

item.title: Season Pass
item.description: Today, let’s set our ambitions for summer reading.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Is a Canadian Car the Answer to Trump’s Tariffs? The Bricklin Shows the Risks.
item.description: A sports car made in New Brunswick and mostly funded by the province lasted only about 18 months.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: The Provincial Archives of New Brunswick
item.media:description: The businessman Malcolm Bricklin and Premier Richard Hatfield at the Bricklin factory in Saint John, New Brunswick. The company’s sports car was funded by the province.

item.title: The World Capital of French Fries Has a Problem: Too Many Potatoes
item.description: Belgium’s potato harvest set a record, just as tariffs hit U.S. demand for frozen fries and as competition from suppliers in Asia intensified.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.media:credit: Kevin Faingnaert for The New York Times

item.title: How Curry Shops Got Caught in Japan’s Immigration Crackdown
item.description: Under Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, new visa rules are forcing some foreign business owners, who have put down roots in Japan, to leave.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández and Kiuko Notoya
item.media:credit: Noriko Hayashi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mahendra Dharmapriya at his Sri Lankan restaurant in Shimotsuke, Japan.

item.title: Iran’s Hard-Liners Try to Derail Potential Deal With the U.S.
item.description: A political fight is playing out in Iran, where the small but loud faction of hard-liners has used rallies, state media and private and public statements to try to undermine negotiations.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard of the late supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, and his successor, Mojtaba Khamenei, during a ceremony in Tehran last Sunday.

item.title: Canadian Man Pleads Guilty to Aiding 14 Suicides
item.description: Kenneth Law, who ran an online business that shipped toxic salt to customers in 40 countries, also admitted to causing the deaths of 79 people in Britain, prosecutors said.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Arlyn Mcadorey/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Kenneth Law, a Canadian man accused of running businesses that shipped toxic salt to people in dozens of countries, pleaded guilty on Friday to 14 counts of aiding suicide in Ontario, prosecutors said.

item.title: Mexican Senate Votes to Allow Voiding Elections Over Foreign Interference
item.description: The legislation, which comes amid growing tensions between Mexico and the White House, must be approved by a majority of state legislatures and sent to the president.
item.dc:creator: Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:credit: Fred Ramos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voting in Mexico City in 2024.

item.title: U.N. Adds Israeli and Russian Forces to Sexual Violence List
item.description: Israel has denied the accusations and condemned the report, which listed dozens of countries and nonstate groups as “credibly suspected” of patterns of rape and sexual violence.
item.dc:creator: Alan Yuhas
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: The northern Gaza Strip in 2025.

item.title: Abdu Rabbu Mansour Hadi, Exiled Ex-President of Yemen, Dies at 80
item.description: In a country fractured by civil war and other crises, he held the title of president from 2012 to 2022 and was widely seen as an ineffective ruler.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:credit: Fethi Belaid/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Abdu Rabbu Mansour Hadi in 2019. A former general and vice president of Yemen, he held the title of president for a decade, leading a weakened, U.S.-backed government.

item.title: Kenya Arrests 8 Students After Fire at School Dorm Kills 16
item.description: The students could be charged next week, Kenya’s education minister said, after the blaze broke out on Thursday at a school run by Kenya’s police.
item.dc:creator: Brian O. Otieno
item.media:credit: Simon Maina/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Detectives and forensic experts outside the Utumishi Girls Academy in Gilgil, Kenya, after the deadly fire on Thursday.

item.title: Israeli Troops Push Deeper Into Lebanon as Cease-Fire Talks Continue
item.description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Israeli ground forces had crossed Lebanon’s Litani River, as military officials from the two countries were set to meet for U.S.-brokered talks.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Daniel Berehulak/The New York Times
item.media:description: An apartment building in Tyre, Lebanon, on Tuesday, in the aftermath of an Israeli strike.

item.title: British Museum Postpones Jewish Cultural Event, Citing Threat of Disruption
item.description: The cancellation comes amid a rise in racial and religious crimes in Britain as well as growing sense of unease for some of London’s Jews.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Sam Bush for The New York Times
item.media:description: The British Museum, where a Jewish cultural event was postponed this week.

item.title: Vietnam’s Leader Warns Asia About the Risks of Superpower Conflict
item.description: In a speech to military leaders from the region, To Lam said that distrust and a lack of respect for established rules had created a culture of “the big fish swallowing the small fish.”
item.dc:creator: Damien Cave
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Edgar Su/Reuters
item.media:description: Vietnam’s leader, To Lam, in Singapore, giving the keynote address on Friday to an annual Asian security conference. While careful not to criticize the United States or China directly, Mr. Lam acknowledged that a series of threats is making it harder for his country and the region to prosper.

item.title: After Orban’s Defeat, Hungary Is Poised to Get Billions From E.U.
item.description: The European Union is set to release 16 billion euros it had withheld, as Hungary moves to enact anti-corruption and rule of law policies.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.media:credit: John Moore/Getty Images
item.media:description: Budapest, Hungary, in early May.

item.title: One of Five Gold Miners Is Rescued From Laos Cave After More Than a Week
item.description: An international effort to free the trapped men brought attention to the country’s informal mines, where panning for gold can pay more than minimum wage.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado and Muktita Suhartono
item.media:credit: Association Of Volunteers For Lao People, via Associated Press

item.title: Romania Says It Could Invoke NATO’s Article 4. What Would That Do?
item.description: The clause in NATO’s founding treaty is an “instrument” Romania can use, the foreign minister said after a drone, alleged to be Russian, wounded two civilians.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross and Aurelien Breeden
item.media:credit: Daniel Mihailescu/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Investigators at the site of a drone crash at an apartment building in Galati, Romania, on Friday.

item.title: What to Know About the Drones That Have Been Crossing Into Romania
item.description: The strike on Friday, near the Ukrainian border, was the first to injure civilians in Romania, officials say. But drones from the nearby war have been a growing threat.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.media:credit: Andreea Campeanu for The New York Times
item.media:description: An apartment building in Galati, Romania, after being struck by a drone on Friday.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Max Bearak

item.title: Court in Kenya Suspends Plans for U.S. Ebola Quarantine Unit
item.description: The order came after U.S. officials said that a 50-bed facility was being established in the African country to house American citizens exposed to the virus.
item.dc:creator: Brian O. Otieno
item.media:credit: Monicah Mwangi/Reuters
item.media:description: Outside the court in Nairobi, the Kenyan capital, that ordered the suspension of the quarantine plans.

item.title: France’s Far Right Has 2 Leaders. They Don’t Always Have One Voice.
item.description: Marine Le Pen and her protégé, Jordan Bardella, lead presidential polls. They are united on curbing immigration but split recently on economic policy.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:credit: Sameer Al-Doumy/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Jordan Bardella and Marine Le Pen at a National Rally event in March. Spotting daylight between them has become a preoccupation for French journalists and analysts.

item.title: Trump’s Boat Strikes Have Failed to Curb Cocaine Flow to U.S., Experts Say
item.description: Despite the rising body count off the South American coast, researchers say cocaine is as easy to get in many parts of the United States as it was before the strikes began.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero
item.category: US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A screen grab from a video posted by U.S. Southern Command this month showed what the U.S. military said was a vessel on a known narco-trafficking route in the eastern Pacific Ocean.

item.title: How Fuel Price Shocks Rattled a Remote New Zealand Village
item.description: The prices of diesel and gasoline soared after the Iran war started, piling more hardship on people in Ruatahuna, one of New Zealand’s most remote villages.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon and Tatsiana Chypsanava
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tatsiana Chypsanava for The New York Times
item.media:description: A public bus runs once a week between Ruatahuna and the city of Rotorua. It is a cheaper option for the villagers who cannot afford to drive because of high fuel prices.

item.title: No, Not That Georgia. A.I. Compounds a Nation’s Identity Woes.
item.description: Online queries are more likely to turn up references to the U.S. state — a problem for the republic in the Caucasus and its people. But a move to drop the name “Georgia” has lost steam.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:credit: Tako Robakidze for The New York Times
item.media:description: Georgia’s ancient, distinctive language and script have long made up part of its national identity.

item.title: Russian Drone Hits Romanian Apartment Building, Officials Say
item.description: Romania is a NATO country, and the security alliance condemned “Russia’s recklessness” for an episode that sharply escalated tensions with Moscow.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Higgins and Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:credit: Andreea Campeanu for The New York Times

item.title: Europe Is Edging Closer to a Trade War With China. Here’s Why.
item.description: As cheap goods pour in, threatening the continent’s manufacturing sector, a search for solutions is becoming increasingly urgent.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek and Alexandra Stevenson
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Chinese-made electric vehicles awaiting export at the port in Hangzhou, eastern China, in May.

item.title: After New Push by the Bolsonaros, U.S. Labels Brazilian Gangs as Terrorist Groups
item.description: Following through on threats, the Trump administration has designated Brazil’s two largest drug gangs as terrorist groups.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova
item.media:credit: Mauro Pimentel/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Members of the Red Command being arrested last year in Rio de Janeiro. The U.S. designated the gang a terrorist group on Thursday.

item.title: Italian Police Uncover Dead Mob Boss’s $230 Million Business Empire
item.description: When Matteo Messina Denaro died in 2023, many of his secrets died with him. That began to change with a tip about a wealthy Sicilian woman’s significant assets in a tiny European principality.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:credit: Carabinieri, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Matteo Messina Denaro being led from a police station after his arrest in Palermo in 2023.

item.title: A Draft U.S.-Iran Plan Is Said to Be on the Table. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: U.S. and Iranian officials say they are closing in on the terms of a preliminary agreement. Yet sticking points, particularly over the Strait of Hormuz, remain.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran depicting the closure of the Strait of Hormuz in May.

item.title: Israel to Cut Ties With U.N. Chief Over Its Inclusion on Sexual Violence Blacklist
item.description: The United Nations secretary general, António Guterres, told Israel there had been an “increasing number of cases” of sexual violence against Palestinian detainees, according to a letter shared by Israel’s U.N. ambassador.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:credit: Charly Triballeau/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Israel’s ambassador to the United Nations, Danny Danon, at U.N. headquarters in March.

item.title: In Strait of Hormuz Standoff, U.S. and Iran Deploy Economic Warfare
item.description: The American blockade and Iran’s control of the Strait of Hormuz have created a stalemate that is neither peace nor raging conflict, with both sides attempting economic strangulation on the water.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kulish and John Ismay
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Vessels anchored at the Strait of Hormuz on Monday. Control of the waterway is a tool Iran has used as leverage against the United States.

item.title: A.I.’s Big Next Step
item.description: Three major companies are planning to go public with share sales in the coming months. They could be some of the biggest market debuts in history.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times

item.title: The Man Turning the Cockroach Into a Gen-Z Movement in India
item.description: Abhijeet Dipke’s “Cockroach Janta Party” has emerged as the unexpected voice of young people feeling let down by the government and struggling to find jobs.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Abhijeet Dipke at a friend’s home in the United States in May. Mr. Dipke launched the satirical digital group Cockroach Janta Party on May 16, after a controversy surrounding a comment by India’s Chief Justice Surya Kant, who referred to unemployed youth as “parasites” and “cockroaches.”

item.title: Austrian Man Convicted of Planning Attack at Taylor Swift Concert in Vienna
item.description: Prosecutors said the defendant plotted in 2024 to set off a bomb outside the concert, which was later canceled. He and another man were also convicted on separate terrorist charges.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:credit: Joe Klamar/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The 21-year-old suspect being escorted by guards to court on Thursday. He was accused of planning an attack on a Taylor Swift concert in Vienna.

item.title: Netanyahu Says Israel Will Control 70 Percent of Gaza, Squeezing Hamas
item.description: Israel’s military continues to conduct strikes and seize territory despite a cease-fire with Hamas. Many of Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu’s supporters see the war as unfinished business.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger and Johnatan Reiss
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Saher Alghorra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Destruction in downtown Gaza City on Thursday.

item.title: The U.S. re-sanctioned a U.N. legal expert critical of Israel.
item.description: Just a few days ago, a U.S. court ordered the Treasury Department to suspend its penalties on Francesca Albanese, whose work on Israel has drawn accusations of antisemitism.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.media:credit: Andrej Isakovic/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Francesca Albanese, the U.N.’s special rapporteur for the Israeli-occupied West Bank and Gaza, in March.

item.title: U.S. and Iran Move Toward Agreement to Reopen the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: President Trump has yet to sign off on an extension of the cease-fire that would allow the two sides to negotiate nuclear and other issues.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman, Jonathan Swan, Tyler Pager and David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: An agreement with Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz would offer President Trump a way out of the war.

item.title: Israel Strikes Beirut, Expanding Offensive in Lebanon
item.description: It was the first attack by Israel on the Lebanese capital in weeks and could complicate efforts by the United States and Iran to agree to a peace deal to end the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Daniel Berehulak/The New York Times
item.media:description: Workers at the scene of a strike in Choueifat, Lebanon, south of Beirut, on Thursday.

item.title: Carney Says That Canada’s Distancing is Good for U.S. in New York Speech
item.description: Speaking in New York, the Canadian prime minister called for a new relationship between Canada and United States.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Angela Weiss/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada speaking in New York on Thursday.

item.title: Pressure Grows on Spain’s Leader After Police Search His Party Office
item.description: After a judge accused his party of corruption, Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez of Spain has faced mounting calls to resign.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:credit: Francesco Fotia/Reuters
item.media:description: Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez of Spain is dealing with a domestic crisis as he has sought to distance himself from a growing aura of corruption among his party and allies.
